html_001: Haruki Murakami · 1987 · 4.3M copies in Japan alone
html_005: Twenty years old, crossing love and death
html_007: “Death is not the opposite of life, but a part of it.”
html_009: A story that starts with a friend’s death at seventeen and walks through the love and loss of turning twenty —
html_011:
is how you’ll learn it.
        It starts easy and keeps going deeper,
html_012: and every question you miss comes back until it clicks.
html_029: That’s the end of the free preview
html_045: and you’ll never forget it. Every road here begins at
html_046: seventeen, the sudden death of Kizuki
html_050: At seventeen, a best friend takes his own life with no warning
html_053: Turning twenty while carrying loss, two people slowly drawing closer
html_055: 🌲 Ami Hostel, a sanatorium in the woods
html_056: Naoko stays in the past; Watanabe keeps watch, coming and going
html_058: 🌻 Midori, a different kind of light
html_059: A heart pulled toward life / Watanabe wavering between two worlds
html_062: “Death isn’t the opposite of life but a part of it” — one phone call back toward living
html_064: Table of contents at a glance
html_068: Focused review of what you missed
html_070: YouTube ‘6-Minute Classics — Norwegian Wood’ explainer video
html_071:
to revisit the core ideas.
      You can also check out Haruki Murakami’s book on the
html_079: A repeat master quiz that turns a book’s core into dopamine
html_080: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s core ideas for study. For the full reading experience, please meet the original work.

js_004: Who wrote this novel, and what’s its original Japanese title?
js_005: Haruki Murakami / Norwegian Wood (ノルウェイの森)
js_007: Osamu Dazai / No Longer Human (人間失格)
js_008: Yukio Mishima / The Temple of the Golden Pavilion (金閣寺)
js_009: Norwegian Wood is a novel Haruki Murakami published in 1987; its original Japanese title is ノルウェイの森. It sold in the millions and turned Murakami into a household name — a phenomenon Japan came to call ‘Murakami fever’.
js_011: Where does the title ‘Norwegian Wood’ come from?
js_012: The title of the Beatles song ‘Norwegian Wood (This Bird Has Flown)’
js_013: A real forest in Norway that Murakami traveled to when he was young
js_014: A place name the author invented, with no real-world origin
js_015: An old nickname for a real forest region in Hokkaido, Japan
js_016: The title comes from the Beatles song ‘Norwegian Wood’. The catch: the song most likely means ‘furniture made of Norwegian wood,’ so rendering it as a ‘forest’ (wood) is, by one well-known account, a title born of a mistranslation.
js_018: When and where is the novel mainly set?
js_019: Late-1960s Tokyo, rippling with student protests
js_020: Osaka at the height of the 1980s bubble-economy boom
js_021: Yokohama during the rapid growth of the 1970s
js_022: Kyoto after the bubble burst in the 1990s
js_023: The core timeline is 1968–1970 Tokyo, stirred by the Zenkyoto student movement. It’s intercut with the present, where a thirty-seven-year-old Watanabe looks back on it all.
js_025: What scene opens the story and starts the flashback?
js_026: A thirty-seven-year-old Watanabe landing at Hamburg Airport, hearing a Beatles song
js_027: Watanabe recalling old friends at his college graduation
js_028: Watanabe looking back on the past at Naoko’s grave
js_029: Watanabe at a wedding reception after years away, suddenly remembering his first love at the sight of a stranger bride
js_030: The novel opens with a thirty-seven-year-old Watanabe landing at Hamburg Airport, hearing an orchestral version of ‘Norwegian Wood’ and being swept up by overwhelming loss and longing. Inside that frame, the story of his early twenties unfolds.
js_032: What is the name and standing of this novel’s narrator and protagonist?
js_033: Toru Watanabe, a literature student at a private university in Tokyo
js_034: Toru Murakami, a law student enrolled at Kyoto University
js_035: Wataru Kobayashi, a repeat exam-taker who came up from the provinces
js_036: Toru Nagasawa, a fourth-year economics student at the University of Tokyo
js_037: The protagonist and narrator is Toru Watanabe. A literature student at a private university in Tokyo, he tells of the love and loss of his early twenties in the first person.
js_039: What happened to Kizuki, Watanabe’s best friend from high school?
js_040: He took his own life at seventeen, leaving no clear reason
js_041: He died suddenly in a traffic accident
js_042: He died after a long illness
js_043: After failing the college entrance exams again and again, he quietly left home and fell out of touch for years
js_044: Kizuki takes his own life at seventeen, by breathing in exhaust fumes inside his car. Leaving no note and no clear reason, this death becomes a lifelong origin point of loss for both Watanabe and Naoko.
js_047: Childhood friends who grew up together, and a couple
js_048: A love interest he first met in the same department at university
js_049: Someone he knew only through letters, as pen pals
js_050: A distant relative he ran into only on holidays
js_051: Naoko and Kizuki were childhood friends who grew up together, and a couple. After Kizuki’s sudden death, Naoko comes to lean on Watanabe — the only other person who knew him.
js_053: How did Watanabe first meet Midori?
js_054: In a drama-history class where they sat side by side at the same university
js_055: As a regular at the record shop where he worked part-time
js_056: Bumping into her by chance at a used bookstore near the dorm
js_057: At a blind-date setup his senior Nagasawa forced on him
js_058: Midori first appears as a classmate in the same drama-history lecture as Watanabe. Unlike the dark, quiet Naoko, Midori is bright, blunt, and brimming with a fierce appetite for life.
js_060: From what point of view is this novel told?
js_061: First-person memory, with Watanabe looking back on his early twenties
js_062: Third-person multiple POV, alternating among several characters
js_063: An omniscient author’s view that knows every character’s inner thoughts
js_064: An epistolary view quoting from Naoko’s diary
js_065: The whole novel is told through Watanabe’s first-person memory. Because the thirty-seven-year-old is tracing memories from his early twenties, the imperfection of memory and a mood of longing soak into every sentence.
js_067: How big was this novel’s commercial reception in Japan?
js_068: It sold roughly 4.3 million copies in Japan alone, sparking what came to be dubbed ‘Murakami fever’
js_069: It was panned on release and went out of print, then was reappraised later
js_070: It caught on abroad first, then became famous back home as if re-imported
js_071: It sold around a hundred thousand copies — a novel only critics talked about
js_072: Since its 1987 release, Norwegian Wood has sold about 4.3 million copies in Japan alone, igniting the so-called ‘Murakami fever’. That explosive popularity became the springboard that lifted Murakami into the ranks of world-famous authors.
js_075: LitCharts · analysis of death and loss themes
js_079: In 1988 it came out under the original title to a lukewarm response; the later re-release under a changed title became the real hit — a fun bit of publishing backstory.
js_080: There’s even debate over what the song itself means — ‘furniture’ or ‘forest’ — so even a single title hides layers of story.
js_081: The noise of the student movement and one person’s private loss crossing in the same time and place — that’s the core function of this historical setting.
js_082: Thanks to the frame narrative, the novel carries a double gaze: ‘me now’ longing for ‘me back then’.
js_083: Because the narrator speaks in the first person, readers meet every other character only through Watanabe’s memory and interpretation.
js_084: Kizuki’s death has no note and no clear motive, which only makes the ‘unexplainability’ of loss stand out more sharply.
js_085: Because Watanabe was the only other person who knew Kizuki, their bond naturally deepens after his death.
js_086: The first-person-memory structure quietly lays a question under the whole novel: ‘Is memory ever accurate?’
js_087: This explosive success was the decisive turning point that made Murakami a world-famous author.
js_088: The film is praised for focusing on translating the original’s lyrical mood into the language of images.
js_093: The path out of the forest
js_103: A thirty-seven-year-old Watanabe looks back on his early twenties — a double gaze that lays memory and longing over the whole novel.
js_104: The start of loss — Kizuki’s death
js_105: A best friend’s sudden suicide at seventeen. With no reason and no note, this death becomes the origin of every character’s sense of loss.
js_106: Naoko — a love that stays in the past
js_107: Unable to let go of Kizuki, she slowly comes apart. She’s the axis that symbolizes ‘death and the past’ in the novel.
js_109: Blunt and bursting with life, she contrasts with the dark, quiet Naoko and stands for ‘life’.
js_110: Ami Hostel — a place of coexistence, not cure
js_111: A retreat in the mountains near Kyoto. It promises no full recovery, but it’s where wounded people keep watch over one another.
js_113: A grown-up carrying her own wounds, she gently soothes Naoko and Watanabe with piano and guitar.
js_115: The hot slogans of Zenkyoto and the reality that soon cools. In the noise of the era, private loss only cuts deeper.
js_116: The critics’ view — sensual scenes and literary standing
js_117: Its frank depictions of sex drew controversy, yet unlike Murakami’s other work, it’s also read as a realist coming-of-age novel.
js_119: In high school, Watanabe and Kizuki are inseparable. The three of them — plus Kizuki’s girlfriend, Naoko — always hang out together.
js_120: One day at seventeen, with no particular reason, Kizuki takes his own life inside his car.
js_121: A death that leaves no note and no clear motive — those left behind are stuck with unanswerable questions.
js_122: Watanabe leaves his hometown for a literature department at a private university in Tokyo, trying for a fresh start.
js_123: Amid unfamiliar streets and faces, he tells himself to avoid forming any deep relationships.
js_125: Reunion with Naoko, and turning twenty
js_126: On a train in Tokyo, Watanabe runs into Naoko by chance. The two slowly grow close again.
js_127: Walking the streets of Tokyo together every Sunday becomes their own quiet ritual.
js_128: On the night of Naoko’s twentieth birthday, the two sleep together for the first time.
js_129: After that night Naoko can’t stop crying, and soon she vanishes from Watanabe’s sight.
js_130: Much later a letter arrives from Naoko — she had gone off to a retreat near Kyoto.
js_132: Midori, a different kind of light
js_133: In the drama-history classroom, Watanabe meets Midori — a bright, unfiltered classmate.
js_134: Unlike Naoko, Midori is blunt and full of life, never hiding her fierce attachment to living.
js_135: Midori’s family runs a small bookstore, and her father is in the hospital, ill.
js_136: Watanabe helps Midori nurse her bedridden father, coming to know a girl who carries life’s weight yet keeps her brightness.
js_137: Midori slowly opens up to Watanabe, but his feelings for Naoko keep him from stepping closer.
js_139: Ami Hostel, the sanatorium in the woods
js_140: Watanabe goes to visit Naoko at ‘Ami Hostel,’ a retreat in the mountains near Kyoto.
js_141: There he meets Naoko’s roommate, Reiko — a former piano teacher, a grown-up with wounds of her own.
js_142: The three walk the forest paths and talk together, spending the most peaceful stretch in the whole novel.
js_143: In the evenings Reiko plays old songs on guitar, the Beatles among them — including ‘Norwegian Wood’.
js_144: Watanabe writes to Naoko, and to one another, trying to keep the bond alive across the distance.
js_146: Things that fall apart, and how to cross loss
js_147: Nagasawa’s girlfriend Hatsumi is kind but always the one who gets hurt. In the end she marries someone else.
js_148: Years later, on hearing that Hatsumi has taken her own life, Watanabe cuts ties with Nagasawa for good.
js_149: Naoko’s condition steadily worsens, and in the end she takes her own life in the woods near Ami Hostel.
js_150: Sunk in grief, Watanabe wanders aimlessly across Japan.
js_151: Reiko leaves Ami Hostel to find Watanabe, and the two hold a quiet farewell rite for Naoko.
js_153: The choice of the one left behind
js_154: After the farewell rite, Watanabe calls Midori from a payphone.
js_155: He tells Midori he wants to start over with her, from the very beginning.
js_156: When Midori asks ‘Where are you right now?’ Watanabe can’t answer at once, trying to gauge where he stands.
js_157: The novel doesn’t declare loss fully overcome; it closes on an open question.
js_158: Carrying the realization that death is not the opposite of life but a part of it, he steps once more toward living.
js_159: The question left by Kizuki’s death
js_160: A seventeen-year-old boy’s death comes with no note and no clear reason. Those left behind never get an answer to ‘why,’ and each carries that loss in their own way. Watanabe and Naoko, the only two who knew Kizuki, try to endure by leaning that silence on each other.
js_161: The fact that not every loss comes with an understandable reason makes us rethink our habit of trying to ‘explain’ grief.
js_162: The night of Naoko’s twentieth birthday
js_163: A single night tangled with joy and loss, desire and guilt. That the moment the two grow closest is also the moment Naoko begins to break shows that feelings never tidy up neatly under a single name.
js_164: Once you accept that joy and sorrow can exist at the same time, your own tangled feelings throw you off a little less.
js_165: The songs Reiko plays for Naoko
js_166: In the evening at Ami Hostel, Reiko plays old songs on guitar, the Beatles among them. It’s a scene where comfort too big for words is replaced by simply sitting together and listening. Later, even after losing Naoko, Reiko plays those songs again with Watanabe, sealing the farewell.
js_167: Mourning doesn’t always have to be completed in words — the very act of being there together can be its own comfort.
js_168: In the face of loss, am I really feeling the grief — or rushing to cover it with an ‘I’m fine’?
js_169: Just as Watanabe takes a long time to finally face his own grief over Kizuki’s death, mourning has no fixed pace.
js_170: Between Naoko (the past · loss) and Midori (the present · life), which way am I walking right now?
js_171: It’s not a question of picking one of them as the ‘right answer,’ but of asking yourself where your heart is turned right now.
js_172: If death exists not as the opposite of life but as a part of it, what will I live out today instead of putting off?
js_173: Bring the novel’s core line into your own today, and a thought about loss becomes a question about how you live right now.
js_174: You fully understood loss and the meaning of life
js_175: A companion on the autumn road
js_176: You caught almost all of the story’s core
js_178: You’ve got the frame — one more time!
js_180: The more you repeat it, the deeper you’ll meet it

_app.title: Norwegian Wood Master Quiz
_app.desc: Haruki Murakami’s Norwegian Wood — the story of crossing loss, in 30 questions
